page: Amazon Web Services c9g.2xlarge is a compute-optimized server instance powered by custom AWS Graviton processors. Built on the arm64 architecture, this instance features 8 dedicated physical cores running at a clock speed of 2.8 GHz with a 1:1 thread-to-core ratio, ensuring no resource sharing at the execution level. It operates on the AWS Nitro hypervisor, which offloads virtualization functions to dedicated hardware to maximize CPU availability for workloads.

The instance is configured with 16.0 GB of system memory, providing a resource density of 2.0 GB per core. It does not include local storage or integrated GPUs, meaning all storage requirements must be met via network-attached block storage. Network connectivity is supported by a baseline bandwidth of 4 Gbps.

Utilizing ARM-based architecture generally offers high resource density and power efficiency compared to traditional x86 alternatives, translating to qualitative cost advantages for compute-bound tasks. The c9g.2xlarge is designed for workloads that benefit from dedicated physical cores and a 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio. Suitable applications include web serving, batch processing, distributed analytics, and containerized microservices that require consistent CPU performance without the overhead of simultaneous multithreading.
description: Amazon Web Services c9g.2xlarge is a compute-optimized server instance utilizing the arm64 architecture and powered by custom AWS Graviton processors. Operating on the AWS Nitro hypervisor, the instance provides 8 dedicated physical cores with a 1:1 thread-to-core ratio running at 2.8 GHz, ensuring dedicated CPU allocation without simultaneous multithreading. It is equipped with 16.0 GB of system memory, yielding a ratio of 2.0 GB per core. The instance features a baseline network bandwidth of 4 Gbps, contains no integrated GPUs, and lacks local storage, relying entirely on network-attached storage options. This hardware profile is designed for compute-bound workloads such as web serving, containerized microservices, and batch processing, offering qualitative cost efficiency inherent to ARM-based architecture and dedicated resource allocation.
